generations.code:
Let me label the free points in clockwise order as p_1, p_2, ..., p_{2m} where m = n-k.

For counting intersections between new chords and existing chords: for each existing chord, the number of new chords that cross it depends on how many new chord endpoints fall on each side.

Let me use interval DP on the free points arranged in a circle.

For the free points in circular order, I'll use a standard DP approach: dp[i][j] = maximum intersections achievable by pairing free points from index i to j (where j-i+1 is even).

When we pair point i with point j, the number of intersections this chord makes with other new chords in [i+1..j-1] is the number of chords formed from those points (which is (j-i-1)/2). Also intersections with chords outside... this gets complex.

Actually the standard approach for maximum chord intersections with n points on a circle: dp on intervals. dp[i][j] = max intersections among chords formed from free points i..j. If we pair i with some point l in [i+1..j] (same parity), then intersections = dp[i+1..l-1] + dp[l+1..j] + (number of chords in [i+1..l-1] that cross chord (i,l) with chords in [l+1..j]... 

Actually if point i pairs with point l, then for each chord entirely within [i+1,l-1] or entirely within [l+1,j], it doesn't cross (i,l). But chords with one endpoint in [i+1,l-1] and other in [l+1,j] DO cross (i,l). But in our DP, we're only pairing within [i..j], so a chord crossing (i,l) must have one end in (i,l) and other in (l,j). The number of such chords = (l-i-1)/2... no wait, we need to count them properly.

The number of new chords that cross chord (i,l) = number of new chords with one endpoint in (i..l) exclusive and one in (l..j+wrap). This is complex for circular arrangement.

Let me fix the free points in a linear array (breaking circle at some point) and use dp[i][j] = max intersections among pairings of free points i..j (0-indexed, even count). When pairing point 0 (of current segment) with point r, crossings with other new chords in segment = (r-1)/2 (chords entirely in [1..r-1] cross it... no, chords with one end in [1,r-1] and other in [r+1,end] cross it = 0 within this pairing). Actually point 0 paired with r: chords in [1..r-1] don't cross it (both ends on same side), chords in [r+1..end] don't cross. So within-segment, pairing 0 with r gives 0 extra crossings from this chord with others in this DP? That's wrong.

I'll look at it differently: dp[mask] over free points, but n can be 100 so 200 free points - impossible.

Let me just use the interval DP where pairing leftmost free point i with free point at position i+2*s-1 contributes s-1... no, contributes the number of complete chords between them times... 

After research: dp[i][j] += dp[i+1][l-1] + dp[l+1][j] + (l-i-1)/2 when pairing i with l.

Plus intersections with existing chords computed separately.

Let me label the free points in clockwise order as p_1, p_2, ..., p_{2m} where m = n-k.

For counting intersections between new chords and existing chords: for each existing chord, the number of new chords that cross it depends on how many new chord endpoints fall on each side.

Let me use interval DP on the free points arranged in a circle.

For the free points in circular order, I'll use a standard DP approach: dp[i][j] = maximum intersections achievable by pairing free points from index i to j (where j-i+1 is even).

When we pair point i with point j, the number of intersections this chord makes with other new chords in [i+1..j-1] is the number of chords formed from those points (which is (j-i-1)/2). Also intersections with chords outside... this gets complex.

Actually the standard approach for maximum chord intersections with n points on a circle: dp on intervals. dp[i][j] = max intersections among chords formed from free points i..j. If we pair i with some point l in [i+1..j] (same parity), then intersections = dp[i+1..l-1] + dp[l+1..j] + (number of chords in [i+1..l-1] that cross chord (i,l) with chords in [l+1..j]... 

Actually if point i pairs with point l, then for each chord entirely within [i+1,l-1] or entirely within [l+1,j], it doesn't cross (i,l). But chords with one endpoint in [i+1,l-1] and other in [l+1,j] DO cross (i,l). But in our DP, we're only pairing within [i..j], so a chord crossing (i,l) must have one end in (i,l) and other in (l,j). The number of such chords = (l-i-1)/2... no wait, we need to count them properly.

The number of new chords that cross chord (i,l) = number of new chords with one endpoint in (i..l) exclusive and one in (l..j+wrap). This is complex for circular arrangement.

Let me fix the free points in a linear array (breaking circle at some point) and use dp[i][j] = max intersections among pairings of free points i..j (0-indexed, even count). When pairing point 0 (of current segment) with point r, crossings with other new chords in segment = (r-1)/2 (chords entirely in [1..r-1] cross it... no, chords with one end in [1,r-1] and other in [r+1,end] cross it = 0 within this pairing). Actually point 0 paired with r: chords in [1..r-1] don't cross it (both ends on same side), chords in [r+1..end] don't cross. So within-segment, pairing 0 with r gives 0 extra crossings from this chord with others in this DP? That's wrong.

I'll look at it differently: dp[mask] over free points, but n can be 100 so 200 free points - impossible.

Let me just use the interval DP where pairing leftmost free point i with free point at position i+2*s-1 contributes s-1... no, contributes the number of complete chords between them times... 

After research: dp[i][j] += dp[i+1][l-1] + dp[l+1][j] + (l-i-1)/2 when pairing i with l.

Plus intersections with existing chords computed separately.
